channel.title: Alpha Funnel — All channels
channel.description: Daily investing, news, and AI & Tools briefings from Alpha Funnel.

item.title: [Investing] Micron gapped up 7% before the bell and Michael Burry is short it, and the space between the invoice and the skeptic is the entire tape this morning
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The memory melt-up ran straight into a famous bear on the first session back from the holiday, the crypto side is bleeding out in silence, and three fresh names (Microsoft, AeroVironment, Veeva) showed up cheap. Here is what to watch into the open.
item.content:encoded:
The memory melt-up ran straight into a famous bear on the first session back from the holiday, the crypto side is bleeding out in silence, and three fresh names (Microsoft, AeroVironment, Veeva) showed up cheap. Here is what to watch into the open.

Two moods are fighting for the tape and they could not be further apart. Semis and memory are gapping up like the supercycle is a settled fact, while crypto is quietly down 50 to 87% from its highs and nobody is talking about it. The morning's job is to hold both in your head without letting either one size your book for you. Here is the open, then the six things that matter.

The open. Futures are green and the memory complex is doing the heavy lifting. Per the Market list, $MU is up about 7% overnight after breaking ground on a $9.3B HBM plant in Hiroshima (high-bandwidth memory, the stacked chips that feed AI accelerators) with $3.1B of Japanese government support, and $SOXX, $SMH, and $SNDK are all set to open firm. Keep yesterday's $SPY map: 755 is the breakout, 740 is the breakdown, per Stocks with Josh.

Three dates own the week. The FOMC minutes land Wednesday July 8 and get read for whether a hike is really coming, the SK Hynix US listing debuts Friday July 10 under $SKHY as a second pure memory play, and $AVAV holds an Investor Day Wednesday July 8. Gold is stalling just above $4,200 as traders scale back Fed hike bets, per the Financial Markets list, and oil is lower on an OPEC+ output increase.

1. The memory melt-up got an invoice and a short-seller in the same session. The bull case keeps getting more concrete. Per the Stocks list, Gavin Baker is now framing memory as the cheapest thing in the AI stack at 3 to 5x earnings, with only three HBM makers on earth and Micron's contracts covering about half its revenue at floor prices above the last cycle's peak margins. And per Lark Davis, Micron's own CEO Sanjay Mehrotra is stretching the runway further, arguing a humanoid robot carries ten times the memory of a self-driving car and that the real multi-decade demand cycle has not even started.

Then the other shoe: Michael Burry has disclosed a short on $MU, and separately, per the Polymarket list, he is calling the whole AI semiconductor complex a bubble about to pop. One live risk to file under the bull case, per the Financial Markets list: China's CXMT is testing next-generation bonded DRAM and closing the gap with Korea faster than expected. That is the one thing that could crack the three-supplier scarcity story.

2. Nvidia's Kyber slipped a year, and that repriced the whole optics supply chain. The under-owned story this morning is a rack delay. Per Melvin, Nvidia's Kyber NVL144 (the 144-chip successor to the current Blackwell rack that Jensen demoed at GTC three months ago) is now reportedly pushed more than 12 months to 2028 on co-packaged optics yield problems, and the NVL72x2 bridge rack was cancelled outright. The read-through: $AMD, $MRVL, and $AVGO all benefit as hyperscalers accelerate their own custom silicon when Nvidia's roadmap slips. The sharper single-name call is $AAOI, which the market treats as a co-packaged optics play but which actually makes most of its money on pluggable transceivers, and a Kyber delay keeps those pluggables bid for two more years.

3. The neoclouds that got crushed on Friday caught a bid overnight. The complex that had its worst two days since COVID is trying to bounce. Per the Stocks list, $NBIS is up about 4% overnight, and $IREN is being called the 2026 bottom on the back of a reported 5-gigawatt, 500,000-GPU Nvidia partnership that puts a $500 target in the conversation, with $CRWV trading near 2.85x forward sales. This is the same high-beta, balance-sheet-sensitive end of the AI trade that broke first on the Meta headline, and a two-day bounce does not fix the leverage. Own it on the flush, never with size into the rip.

4. Microsoft is the cheapest it has been in five years, and the drawdown is almost entirely multiple. A fresh name worth real attention. Per one detailed Reddit breakdown, $MSFT sits near $373 to $390 at a 19.3x forward multiple, roughly 40% below its three-year average, with the entire 33% drawdown coming from multiple compression rather than any business crack. The fundamentals are still elite: Azure grew 40%, the AI business is at a $37B annualized run rate up 123% year over year, operating margin is 46%, and return on equity is 34%.

Per a second write-up, a base-case DCF lands fair value around $580 to $600, though the honest risk is the roughly $190B of 2026 capex crushing free cash flow, plus a restructured OpenAI deal that ended Azure's model exclusivity. Earnings hit July 29.

5. Bitcoin's bottom call split cleanly in two overnight. You have two credible reads pointing opposite ways, and that split is the whole decision. Per Benjamin Cowen, the four-year cycle is replaying 2018 at exactly 10x the price, the local low of $57,000 already printed on July 1, and a July window of strength should carry Bitcoin back toward its 200-day average before it rolls over into a Q4 low. Against that, per Stocks with Josh, the bottom is not in, $69,000 is the level that invalidates the downtrend, and until price closes above it he wants to short the rejection there.

Under both calls the damage is already brutal. Per Wajahat Mughal, Bitcoin is down 50% from its high, Ether 64%, and the alts are a graveyard with $SOL down 72% and $AAVE down 87%. The long-term case has not changed, and per Jeff Park Bitcoin has still existed for only about 7% of the time the United States has, but that is cold comfort to anyone marked to market this morning.

6. Saylor sold Bitcoin for the first time in four years, and the preferred is where the stress shows. The MicroStrategy story turned genuinely two-sided. Per Lark Davis, $MSTR has fallen to an $82 two-year low even though it still holds 847,000 Bitcoin worth roughly $55 to $60B, and Saylor sold a token 32 coins, likely for compliance reasons, while authorizing a larger monetization program. The real pressure is in the $STRC preferred, which trades at $71 against a promised $100 as its dividend obligations quadrupled from $300M to $1.2B in six months and coverage runway collapsed from seven years to fourteen months.

The bull answer is Saylor's own math that those dividends survive 40 years even at zero Bitcoin appreciation, but Peter Schiff is calling it finished and JP Morgan now labels him a risk to Bitcoin itself.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $AVAV (AeroVironment). Per this earnings breakdown, Q4 revenue came in at $641.6M, up 133% year over year and well past consensus, adjusted EPS was $1.84 versus $1.47 expected, and the stock ripped about 35% in three sessions before landing a $500M firm Army contract for the Titan counter-drone system. Investor Day is Wednesday July 8, the first look at strategy after the BlueHalo merger. Consensus target sits near $258 against a roughly $191 price.
  • $SKHY (SK Hynix). The US listing debuts Friday July 10, a second pure memory play beside Micron into a tape that is already gapping the sector up. If you want memory exposure without single-name Micron risk, this is the new instrument to size.
  • $VEEV (Veeva Systems). Per this write-up, the life-sciences software vendor owns more than 80% of pharma CRM seats, carries zero debt and over $5B net cash, and has compounded free cash flow per share above 35% a year while the market has left it unloved. No catalyst named, a watch-and-wait, but a quality name going cheap.
  • $PFE (Pfizer). Per Stocks with Josh, a fresh swing idea with a stated entry near $23.50, trading below its pre-COVID price at a 6 to 7% dividend with the revenue-cliff fear largely priced in. A slow accumulate, not a lump-sum chase.
  • $SOFI. Per the People-to-follow list, the loan-platform take rate is running near 4.6% on about $12B annualized originations at roughly 15x 2028 earnings with 30%-plus member growth. Reports July 29.

Psychology. Look at what the tape is asking you to feel this morning. Memory and semis are gapping up, the timeline is full of people posting $1,800 and $3,000 targets on $MU and $SNDK, and the pull is to chase the green candle before the bell so you do not miss it. That is exactly the moment Michael Burry chose to disclose a short and call the whole AI-semi complex a bubble, and per the Crypto list even Pentosh1 admitted he did not buy the memory dip on Friday. When the crowd is euphoric and one of the few people who called the last blowup is fading it, the risk is not being wrong on the thesis. The risk is oversizing into strength because the strength feels safe.

Now look at the other screen. Crypto is down 50 to 87% across the board, Saylor is selling for the first time in four years, and per the Crypto list Novogratz is saying nobody cares about crypto anymore. That silence is the opposite emotion, the flat apathy that shows up near lows and makes you want to close the tab and stop looking.

The discipline today is to run both feelings through the same rule. Per Steve Burns, risk a fixed unit per trade and count your outcomes in R, not dollars, so a hot memory name and a bombed-out alt are the same size of bet and neither the euphoria nor the despair gets to move your hand. The people who blow up in a tape like this are not the ones who pick wrong. They are the ones who felt certain and let the feeling set the size.

item.title: [News] A joint rebel-jihadist offensive hit Mali's army and its Russian mercenaries on the Fourth of July, and America's 2028 race is already taking shape a year early
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Sahel militants coordinated an attack that killed around 20 in Sevare, JD Vance pulled clear of the Republican field, and Britain's politics flipped as Labour edged back ahead of Reform. Here is what moved over the holiday.
item.content:encoded:
Sahel militants coordinated an attack that killed around 20 in Sevare, JD Vance pulled clear of the Republican field, and Britain's politics flipped as Labour edged back ahead of Reform. Here is what moved over the holiday.

The holiday weekend was quiet at home and busy at the edges. A coordinated offensive in the Sahel put Russia's African footprint back in the headlines, the American 2028 field started to harden a full year before anyone votes, and Britain's two-party fight swung again. Here is what happened.

1. Militants hit Mali's army and its Russian mercenaries in a coordinated Fourth of July offensive. The Sahel escalation is the real news of the weekend. Per the Polymarket list, a separatist force and the al-Qaeda-linked JNIM launched a joint offensive on July 4 against Mali's ruling junta and the Africa Corps mercenaries who prop it up, with around 20 people reported killed in the central town of Sevare. The detail that matters is the coordination. A Tuareg separatist movement and a jihadist group are ideological rivals, and when they attack the same target together it points at a junta losing control of its own center, not just its periphery. It also drags Moscow in, because the Africa Corps presence is the thing keeping this government standing.

2. The 2028 American field is already hardening, and Vance is pulling clear. A year out, the prediction markets are starting to converge. Per the Kalshi list, JD Vance now sits at 41% to be the 2028 Republican nominee, up from 32% a month ago, the kind of move that signals a field consolidating around a front-runner rather than splintering. The down-ballot action is in Michigan, where the same market has the Democratic primary reshuffling hard: Abdul El-Sayed slipped from 90% to 80%, Haley Stevens doubled from 11% to 22%, and the odds of Republicans taking the Senate seat at all climbed from 19% to 30%. Early markets are noisy, but a 10-point swing in a month is a real shift in who the serious money thinks wins.

3. Britain's politics flipped back, with Labour edging ahead of Reform. The UK story is a reversal. Per the Kalshi list, Labour has climbed to 37% in the next-general-election market and overtaken Nigel Farage's Reform at 33%, unwinding the Reform lead that had defined British polling for months. It is a market read rather than a ballot, so treat it as sentiment, but the direction is the point. The insurgent-right surge that looked inevitable through the spring has stalled, and the governing party is clawing back the lead it was supposed to have lost for good.

4. Around the world, quickly.

  • Hungary. Per the Polymarket list, a bill to remove President Tamas Sulyok, an Orban ally, has the market at 68% for an ouster this month, a rare and serious move against the ruling bloc.
  • Taiwan. Per the same list, Taipei is flagging a jump in Chinese naval activity around the island alongside joint China-Russia drills, the kind of tempo increase that gets watched for a pattern rather than a one-off.
  • Ukraine. Per the Financial Markets list, Ukrainian long-range strikes have now disrupted an estimated 15 to 30% of Russia's refining capacity, a slow attritional campaign aimed at the fuel behind the front.
  • Oil. Per the same list, Saudi Arabia pushed crude exports to a record near 5 million barrels a day as OPEC+ lifts output, and prices fell on the extra supply.
  • South Korea. Per the Polymarket list, Seoul is beginning 24-hour trading in the won, a structural step toward treating its currency like a global one.
  • United States. Per the Financial Markets list, the first joint New York Stock Exchange and Nasdaq bell-ringing from the Oval Office is set for Monday morning to mark the launch of Trump Accounts.

item.title: [AI & Tools] DeepSeek's big drop is a speed patch, not a smarter model, and the more interesting AI story this morning is who quietly owns the agent stack
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: DSpark turns out to be a serving-layer trick on the same V4 weights, the Nous Hermes tooling is maturing into a real platform, and a claim is circulating that Washington briefly cut Fable 5 and Mythos 5 off for everyone. Here is the signal.
item.content:encoded:
DSpark turns out to be a serving-layer trick on the same V4 weights, the Nous Hermes tooling is maturing into a real platform, and a claim is circulating that Washington briefly cut Fable 5 and Mythos 5 off for everyone. Here is the signal.

The headline names today are DeepSeek and a policy scare, but the durable story is underneath. The value in AI keeps sliding from raw model capability toward the plumbing that runs agents cheaply and the judgment to point them at the right work. Here is what actually changed.

1. DeepSeek DSpark is a speed patch, and the number that matters is not the one being quoted. Read the model card before the hype. Per Kris Torrington, DSpark is not a new brain, it is the same DeepSeek V4 checkpoint with a speculative decoding module bolted on (a small draft model guesses the next tokens and the big model verifies them), so this is a serving upgrade, not a capability jump.

  • Reported speedups: V4 Flash runs about 60 to 85% faster per user, V4 Pro about 57 to 78% faster.
  • The specs underneath: V4 Flash is 284B parameters with 13B active, V4 Pro is 1.6 trillion with 49B active, both at a million-token context.
  • The catch: faster tokens do not mean your bill drops 85%. The only metric that pays you back is cost per completed workflow, and Torrington's own bar for switching is a 20 to 30% improvement on that, not a 5% one.

2. The Nous Hermes stack is turning into a real agent platform while nobody was looking. The open-agent side keeps shipping, and it is past toy stage. Per the Hermes list, two pieces landed that matter more than another benchmark tweet.

  • A Hermes browser extension hit v2: full session management, vision and screenshots, a side panel on every tab, and the ability to swap models mid-chat between Opus, Grok, GPT, and a local model. Eight-plus releases in a week.
  • A walkthrough of the Hermes Agent architecture showed the guts: 70-plus tools across roughly 28 toolsets, 18-plus providers, a 90-turn iteration budget, subagents capped at 50, and preflight context compression that kicks in at 50% full. It reads context files in priority order, checking its own `.hermes.md` before `AGENTS.md` before `CLAUDE.md`.

3. The local-model crowd is not a hobby anymore, it is a staffing model. The most interesting shift in the list is cultural. Per the Hermes list, Alex Finn described running four models across six computers 24/7 doing security scans, code optimization, scraping, and agent monitoring, and framed always-on local compute as unlimited Opus-level intelligence you do not meter. He still reaches for Fable 5 and Codex on the hard stuff, and that admission is the honest part. The takeaway from yesterday holds and sharpens: route the cheap and endless work to local swarms, and save the expensive frontier calls for the problems only you could have imagined.

4. Read the bayes near-future thread for the non-obvious bets, not the "AI will accelerate" part. A long forecast thread made the rounds, and most of it is the usual takeoff talk. Per bayes, the parts worth your attention are the specific ones.

  • The central wager: 4 to 7 more orders of magnitude of intelligence output per unit of scale are still on the table, which would make the current flat-looking frontier curve very misleading.
  • A compute-allocation squeeze that hits open source hardest: are your GB300s more valuable serving GLM 5.2 or building the next model inside a frontier lab? Capital does not fix it if the chips go to the frontier first.
  • A robotics ChatGPT-moment followed by an Opus-grade moment, with the gap between them measured in months, and physical scale-up toward roughly 100 million robots a year by 2030.

5. A policy scare to keep at arm's length, because the sourcing is thin. Handle this one carefully. Per Kris Torrington, the US government told Anthropic to restrict Fable 5 and Mythos 5 from foreign nationals, and because nationality could not be verified in real time, the models briefly went dark for everyone before access was restored. It advances yesterday's thread about Washington possibly pulling access to a major model, and it fits the new prediction markets on exactly that question. The honest note: this comes from a single creator with no named official, agency, or confirmation attached, so treat it as a claim to watch rather than an established fact.

The point that survives either way is the real one. If your whole stack sits on one frontier API, it can be working in the morning and cut off by policy that afternoon, and that is a dependency risk you can actually plan around. On a related thread, per the Market list, one investor is now betting against the standalone cybersecurity vendors on the theory that coding models are quietly eating their moat.

item.title: [Investing] Micron was the whole week, and by Sunday the trade had a famous bear, a structural bull, and a number under $1,000 to explain
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The AI memory trade ran through all eight editions this week: record fundamentals and a supplier now invoicing 20% price hikes on one side, Michael Burry's chart short and a crowded-buy flush on the other. Here is the full case, and what to actually do with it.
item.content:encoded:
The AI memory trade ran through all eight editions this week: record fundamentals and a supplier now invoicing 20% price hikes on one side, Michael Burry's chart short and a crowded-buy flush on the other. Here is the full case, and what to actually do with it.

Every trading day this week had memory at the center of it, and no other story came close. Micron opened the week getting flushed under $1,000, spent the middle of it absorbing a Michael Burry short, and closed it with a supplier literally invoicing a 20% price hike and a decade-scoreboard showing memory as the best-performing corner of the entire market. This is the one position the whole book keeps circling, so it earns the long form. The bull case and the bear case are both real, they are arguing about different things, and knowing which is which is the difference between owning this well and getting run over in it.

Why Micron is the week's subject and not just another semi in the AI basket. The tell is that the same name led the tape from two opposite directions. On Monday the memory air pocket showed up and $MU gave back 6.7% in a session. By Wednesday Micron had closed under $1,000 and every desk that bought the dip looked early. Then on Thursday Michael Burry opened a short and the entire complex wobbled, and by Friday the bulls had answered with a record print and Asia had voted with a green tape. A stock does not do that unless it is the fulcrum of the market's biggest live debate. It is.

The bull case, in full. Strip out the noise and the long side rests on four legs, and they are stronger than the price action this week suggested.

  • The supply is structurally short, and management keeps saying so on the record. CEO Sanjay Mehrotra told Bloomberg that Micron can meet only about 50% to two-thirds of key-customer demand right now, that the shortage runs well beyond 2026 with no meaningful new supply until 2028, and that the take-or-pay book, the contracts where the buyer pays whether or not it takes the chips, is up to 16 agreements with $GM freshly added and 14 of them carrying a $100B minimum-revenue floor. He is committing $200B to a US buildout on the back of it.
  • The margins are the kind you see once a cycle. The most recent quarter ran an 84.9% gross margin, and Wolfe Research pegs Micron's DRAM gross margins above 90%. The reason it is durable is physical: high-bandwidth memory eats four times the wafer capacity of standard DDR5, so adding wafers does not add bits, and the clean-room shortage caps supply well into 2027.
  • The valuation still prices a cyclical, not a monopoly. Fiscal Q3 revenue was $41.5B, up 346% year over year, with adjusted EPS of $25.11 against a $20.49 estimate, and the Q4 guide is $50B in revenue and $31 EPS. Against a CEO target of $4,000 the stock has traded near 6x forward earnings. That is a value multiple stapled to a hypergrowth print.
  • The forecast became an invoice. The single most important development of the week was that the thesis stopped being a thesis. Samsung began issuing verbal notices to raise DRAM prices roughly 20% quarter over quarter for Q3. A price hike you model is a hope. A price hike your supplier sends in writing is a fact, and it flows straight to Micron's line.

The smart-money vote backs the fundamentals. Martin Shkreli made the cleanest structural argument, that DRAM is not a commodity because it needs cleanroom fabs full of $ASML and Lam Research machines nobody can stand up on demand, so rational entrants match the incumbent price instead of starting a war, and memory behaves like a disciplined oligopoly. Leopold Aschenbrenner is voting with real weights, 14.9% of his book in $SNDK and more in the memory-and-power complex. And the 2026 scoreboard says they have been right all year: $SNDK up 635%, $MU up 242%, $WDC up 213%, $MRVL up 189%. Memory is not participating in this market, it is leading it.

The bear case, in full, and it is not what most people think. The mistake is treating the Burry short as a fundamental call, when it is nothing of the kind. Per the Market list, Burry's Substack short is pure technical analysis, Micron overextended from its 200-day average targeting roughly a 30% drawdown, with nothing about HBM or supply under it. That is a bet on gravity and positioning rather than on the business.

And on that narrow point he might be right, because the real bear case this week was crowding. This was, in the words of Tuesday's edition, the most crowded buy on the board, the rare moment when every independent desk piled onto the same trade at the same time, and crowds like that either front-run a move or mark a local top. The stock closing under $1,000 mid-week, then the concentrated flush that took $SNDK down 14.8% and $MRVL down 11.2% in a session, is what an overowned trade does when it exhales.

The two legitimate fundamental worries are about timing rather than thesis. The first is the 2028 air pocket, the point where the new supply Mehrotra is building finally lands and the shortage eases, a real risk to the out-years even if it does not bite in 2026. The second is macro. The Fed turned hawkish enough this week that a July hike went from unthinkable to a coin flip under Kevin Warsh, and Tom Lee mapped a September-to-October correction driven by the Anthropic and OpenAI IPO supply shock. A high-beta, heavily-owned name like Micron is exactly what gets sold first in that kind of drawdown, regardless of how good the invoice looks.

The verdict, and what to do with it. My conviction is that the bull case is the stronger one and the bear case is mostly about the entry, not the asset. When the supplier is invoicing the price hike, the CEO is turning away a third of his demand, and the stock trades at 6x forward, you are being paid to own the structural short in memory. But Burry is shorting the crowd, and a tape this one-sided can hand him his 30% on positioning alone before the fundamentals win. So the play is to own the core position through the noise and treat any Fed-driven or IPO-supply-driven flush as the place to add rather than the reason to exit.

  • Levels that matter. Stocks with Josh put a measured-move target of $1,500 on Micron, activating on a reclaim of $1,250, with a hard bounce expected first off the $895 fibonacci level. That $895-to-$1,250 zone is the accumulation range if the autumn correction Tom Lee flagged actually arrives.
  • The watchlist around it. The bottleneck arc runs compute to memory to connectivity, so the same trade lives in $MRVL on the connectivity leg, $SNDK and $WDC on the storage leg, and the neoclouds that rent the compute, $NBIS above all, which kept signing capacity all week. $INTC is the wild card, up 226% on the year as the only leading-edge US foundry with its 18A yield reportedly fixed.
  • The risk control. Size it like the high-beta position it is and never like a core holding you can forget about. The thing that breaks this trade is not a bad Micron quarter, it is a Warsh hike or the IPO air pocket dragging every crowded AI name down together. Keep dry powder for that, because that is when you want to be buying the invoice, not selling the shakeout.

item.title: [News] The unemployment rate says the labor market is fine, and almost every other number this week says it is quietly coming apart
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: AI got named in 101,743 job cuts, the household survey shows well over a million jobs gone this year, and workers aged 22 to 25 are down 13% since the models got good. This was the week the AI jobs squeeze stopped being a forecast.
item.content:encoded:
AI got named in 101,743 job cuts, the household survey shows well over a million jobs gone this year, and workers aged 22 to 25 are down 13% since the models got good. This was the week the AI jobs squeeze stopped being a forecast.

The loudest number in economics is the one hiding the story. The headline unemployment rate sat near 4.2% all week and the recession bet kept losing, and underneath both, four separate editions surfaced the same quiet crack: artificial intelligence is starting to reprice who is employable, it is landing hardest on the young and the entry-level, and the topline data is built to miss it. This is the story that will define the politics of the next two years more than any monthly jobs print, so it is worth pulling the week's threads into one place.

What actually happened this week. The evidence arrived in four waves, each from a different source, each pointing the same direction.

  • AI became the single biggest named reason for layoffs. The Challenger layoff data walked through on ClearValue Tax put AI behind 101,743 job cuts year to date, about 23% of all cuts and 31% of June's alone, up from low single digits historically. Tech as a sector accounts for roughly a third of all cuts this year, 139,156 and up 83%. That is a category that essentially did not exist as a layoff reason two years ago.
  • The household survey says the jobs are actually gone. Per both the Polymarket and Financial Markets feeds, the household survey shows the US has shed more than 1.7 million jobs so far this year, pushing employment to its lowest since December 2024, even as the odds of a recession this year stayed near record lows. By Saturday the Polymarket list added that more than 1.8 million Americans are now averaging at least 27 weeks of unemployment, the long-term-jobless measure that tends to move last and matter most.
  • The damage is concentrated on the young. The clearest signal came from a Stanford and ADP study relayed on the Crypto list: workers aged 22 to 25 in AI-exposed jobs are down 13% since late 2022, recent-graduate unemployment sits at 5.6% against a 4.3% national rate, and 42.5% of graduates are underemployed. The machine is eating the bottom rung of the ladder first, the exact jobs a new worker used to climb through.
  • Fear caught up to the data. By the weekend the Polymarket list reported half of all Americans now say they fear AI could cost them or a family member a job, and in a detail that tells you where the displacement runs, AI hiring in India's IT sector is rising even as overall recruitment there falls. The tool is not destroying work evenly. It is moving it.

The counterweight, because it is real. The honest version of this story includes the other side. The same Stanford and ADP data shows that firms adopting AI early are still adding people, with Ramp data putting AI-adopter headcount up 10.2% and entry-level roles up 12% at those firms. So the picture is not "AI destroys jobs," it is a divergence: the companies that wield the tool are hiring, and the workers and firms that do not are being hollowed out. That gap, adopters pulling ahead while everyone else sheds, is the actual shape of what is happening, and it is a more uncomfortable story than a simple jobs apocalypse because it has winners standing right next to the losers.

The frame the week kept reaching for. Two of the sharpest cultural takes of the week were really about this same divide. David Friedberg's makers-versus-takers immigration test argued that the only question that matters is whether a person comes to produce or to consume, and his faith that any productive person grows the economy runs headfirst into a labor market that is quietly deleting the entry-level rungs where productivity used to start. Scott Galloway's creator-economy read from Cannes, that brand spend is up 24% while the money flows to nano and micro creators and "the most takes the most," is the same winner-take-more dynamic in a different industry. The through-line across immigration, media, and the jobs data is a single unsettling idea: the economy is bifurcating into those who direct the tools and those the tools direct around.

What to watch next. Three things will tell you whether this is a soft patch or a structural break. First, watch the divergence between the establishment survey and the household survey. The headline rate is smoothing over a household-level contraction, and when those two finally reconcile, the reconciliation is the news. Second, watch the graduate class of 2026 hit the market this summer, because a 5.6% recent-grad unemployment rate and 42.5% underemployment is the leading edge, and it either eases as adopters keep hiring or it hardens into a lost cohort. Third, watch the politics. A labor market that looks fine on paper while half the country fears for its job is a combustible gap, and it is the kind of gap that gets legislated, taxed, or campaigned on well before the official statistics admit there is a problem. The number to distrust for the next two years is the unemployment rate. The numbers to trust are the ones underneath it.

item.title: [AI & Tools] Fable 5 came back from an 18-day ban as the best model on the board, wrapped in guardrails that started a revolt, and it goes paid on Monday
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The whole week was one story: the return of Claude Fable 5. What it can do is a genuine step change, how it is gated made power users furious, and the free window closes July 7. Here is the full arc and how to use it before the meter starts.
item.content:encoded:
The whole week was one story: the return of Claude Fable 5. What it can do is a genuine step change, how it is gated made power users furious, and the free window closes July 7. Here is the full arc and how to use it before the meter starts.

One model dominated every edition this week. Claude Fable 5 came back online after an 18-day export ban, and its return set off the whole chain of stories that followed: a capability leap the practitioners are calling real, a safety-gating backlash from the people who use it hardest, a pricing change that closes the free window on July 7, and a durable lesson about how to build so that no single model outage or price hike can hurt you again. If you only internalize one AI story from the week, this is it.

The arc, start to finish. The timeline matters, because the drama was in the sequence.

  • The ban and the return. Per AI Central, Fable 5 shipped June 9, was hit with a US export ban June 12, and came back to global availability July 1 after 18 days dark. The following day US Commerce formally lifted the export controls, which reframed the model as something closer to a national-security asset than a product launch. It returned wired with new cybersecurity classifiers and a routing change where routine coding requests can silently fall back to Opus 4.8.
  • The revolt. The gating is what the community actually reacted to. On the Hermes list, one heavy user called it "not nerfed, it is SLAUGHTERED," another reported it hardblocking legitimate biology work, and the safety classifiers meant to route sensitive tasks to Opus were catching ordinary requests. The most useful reframe came from Alex Finn, who argued Fable 5 is better understood as an operating-system-level tool than a chat model, so you build around it rather than talk to it.
  • The capabilities, once you get past the guardrails. This is the part that justifies the noise. The Limitless podcast detailed what the model does when you clear the classifiers: it now has what they call visual intelligence, an actual sense of aesthetic taste, so the artifacts it builds stop looking like AI slop. One demo built a fully playable Hogwarts game with physics, sound, and spatial awareness from a single prompt in one shot. And Claude Science, a research-tuned version with a tool harness, is producing 100-plus-page literature reviews for the Allen Institute in hours instead of what used to take up to two years.
  • The practitioner verdict. The people who ship agents for a living are the most convincing witnesses. On the Hermes list, Shannon Sands and Uzi called Fable 5 the first model that makes long-horizon agents feel reliable, Thariq published a "Field Guide" for driving it, and the concrete example is Machina's deck-building agent posting roughly 3x gains once it leaned on the model's memory instead of stuffing everything into the prompt.

The economics, and why Monday matters. The free ride is short. Per Nate Herk, Fable 5 is priced at $10 per million input tokens and $50 per million output, which is 2x Opus, the promotional period ends July 7, and on Claude plans you can only spend up to 50% of your weekly limits on Fable before it flips to usage credits. His honest read is that you realistically need Fable 5 for only 5% to 15% of your work, the genuinely hard long-horizon tasks, so the move this weekend is not to run it on everything but to point it at the few problems that actually need it. The Chase AI cost breakdown adds the lever that matters: the model's price swings enormously with its effort setting, the dial that controls how much reasoning it burns per task, so on a paid meter the effort dial is your main cost control.

The lesson that outlasts the model. The most valuable takeaway is not about Fable 5 at all. It is the thing the 18-day ban taught. Per Nate B Jones, the firms that shrugged off the outage were the ones that own their harness, the scaffolding and tooling around a model rather than its raw intelligence, and simply rerouted to another model when Fable went dark. That is the discipline this whole episode should install: keep your harness model-agnostic so the next ban, price hike, or release is a config change instead of a migration.

The corollary Jones drew on the jobs side is worth holding too. The model does not run itself. The only roles it actually eliminates are the pure-execution ones with zero judgment. Everything else still needs a person to aim it, feed it context, check its work, and own the result, a role he calls the model manager. And as the model gets more capable, that aiming becomes the skill. The best takeaway of the week came from Dylan Davis: with a model this eager, your old maximalist prompts now backfire, and the new skill is restraint, telling it the destination and what done looks like, then getting out of its way.

What to watch next. Three things. First, July 7, when the free window closes and behavior changes, because a model you cannot afford to run in a loop is a demo, and the effort dial becomes the whole game. Second, GPT-5.6, which the market priced to land as soon as this week, arriving directly into the Fable comparison, though beware that early benchmarks were run against a Mythos preview and different harnesses, so the head-to-head claims will not line up cleanly. Third, whether Anthropic loosens the classifiers, because right now the biggest thing standing between Fable 5 and its own capabilities is not a competitor, it is its own guardrails. Build model-agnostic, spend the effort dial deliberately, and use the free days you have left on the hard problems.

item.title: [Investing] The memory trade got its invoices this weekend, and on the very same tape the companies that rent out the compute had their worst two days since COVID
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: UBS put hard numbers on the DRAM price hikes and Micron locked five-year take-or-pay contracts through 2030, while the neoclouds fell 20 to 25% in two sessions on one Meta headline. The AI trade is splitting into the part you get paid to own and the part that just got dangerous.
item.content:encoded:
UBS put hard numbers on the DRAM price hikes and Micron locked five-year take-or-pay contracts through 2030, while the neoclouds fell 20 to 25% in two sessions on one Meta headline. The AI trade is splitting into the part you get paid to own and the part that just got dangerous.

Two things happened at once and they point in opposite directions. The memory supercycle stopped being a forecast and became a set of signed contracts and quoted price hikes. At the same moment, the equities built on renting AI compute went through the fastest drawdown in years on a single Meta headline. Owning the AI trade now means knowing which half you are in. Here is the week ahead and the split underneath it.

The week ahead. Markets reopen Monday July 6 after the long weekend. Three dates matter. The FOMC minutes land Wednesday July 8 and get read for whether a July hike is real. The SK Hynix Nasdaq listing arrives Friday July 10 under $SKHY, a second pure memory play beside $MU with a 91% DRAM gross margin last quarter. And earnings season opens the following week, with $SOFI and $HOOD both reporting July 29. Per Stocks with Josh, SPY still runs on 755 as the breakout and 740 as the breakdown, with a note worth holding: July averages up 2.5%, but every fourth year July is weak, and 2026 sits on that cycle.

1. The memory trade got its invoices, and that changes the argument. This morning the memory story was a thesis about scarcity. Tonight it is paper. Per the Market list, UBS now models DDR contract prices up 32% in Q3 and 18% in Q4, above its own prior 17% and 12% estimates, with NAND up 30% quarter over quarter and Samsung pushing a 20% DRAM hike. Micron is leading a fresh 1.5 trillion yen, roughly $9.3B, Hiroshima buildout.

Per the Milk Road Micron breakdown, the structure under it is the tell: 16 strategic customer agreements, five-year take-or-pay running through 2030, customers have already posted $22B in cash deposits against a roughly $100B backlog, and about half of company revenue is heading under contract. On a PEG screen, per the Market list, $MU and $SNDK both price near 0.1x growth while $TSLA sits at 4.5x. The one caution worth respecting: per the People-to-follow list, LuxAlgo is already flagging memory stocks as trading in a bear market even as the fundamentals scream, and that gap between the invoice and the chart is the whole trade.

2. The neoclouds just had their worst two days since COVID, and the reason matters more than the move. Per the Crypto list, the neocloud complex fell 20 to 25% across two straight sessions, described as the worst two-day momentum selloff since COVID, triggered by Meta signaling it will rent out excess data-center capacity. Per Joseph Hogue, that pulled $NBIS, $CRWV, $IREN, and $ORCL down 20 to 35% in two weeks, and he lays out the deeper worry: these companies borrow heavily, their hardware is obsolete in about two years, and if Meta and SpaceX both dump spare capacity into the market, a default wave is not impossible.

The bull answer is that the demand is real and this is a gift, and per the Crypto list Meta is still spending $125B to $145B on infrastructure in 2026 and signaling it remains compute-constrained. My read: this is the high-beta, balance-sheet-sensitive end of the AI trade, and it is exactly the part that breaks first when the story wobbles. Own it small or own it on the flush, never with size into strength.

3. TSMC is quietly repricing the entire AI supply chain. The under-covered lever this weekend is not a chip, it is a price list. Per Rick Orford, $TSM is raising 3nm wafers as much as 15% and pricing its new 2nm node near $30,000 a wafer, the highest ever, into a sold-out order book. Because the fabs and staff are already paid for, repriced nodes running at about 74% of a $100B-plus revenue base flow almost straight to earnings, pointing at a 10 to 15% profit uplift on top of volume. The risk to name: an ITC investigation from two patent firms is seeking an exclusion order on chips built at 7nm and smaller, with $TSM, $AAPL, $QCOM, and $AVGO named, a preliminary ruling due within about a month and a final decision in October. Low odds, but a binary date on the calendar.

4. Bitcoin's bottom debate turned more bearish, and the ETF story is the reason. The dueling targets sharpened into a real spread. Per Milk Road, the three houses now split cleanly: Galaxy says no bottom yet with a $40K target, NYDIG says maybe with a roughly $50K read, and Standard Chartered says the bottom is already in at $59K.

Under it sits a structural drain. Per the Milk Road letter, James Seyffart of Bloomberg Intelligence calls Bitcoin ETP diamond hands a myth, with the products now more than $11B below their October peak and about $700M leaving in a single day on June 25, capital getting pulled toward AI and space. Per the Financial Markets list, Rekt Capital notes BTC has deviated below its 200-week average and expects a July relief bounce before a deeper flush toward the final bear-market bottom. The one bright spot is a decoupling: per Milk Road, the Bitwise crypto-equities index is up about 30% this year while Bitcoin is down about 30%, which says the equities, names like $COIN and $CRCL, are becoming the cleaner way to hold the theme than the coin.

5. The macro fight is now explicit: is AI inflationary or not? This morning the Fed picture was two-sided. Tonight both sides showed up with data. Per the Financial Markets list, Morgan Stanley argues the case that AI is disinflationary should be re-examined and possibly rejected, because faster productivity lifts the neutral rate and undercuts the reason to cut. Against that, per Jordi Visser, the market has priced out cuts and priced in roughly three hikes while the Cleveland Fed nowcast points to a slightly negative June CPI print and July near -0.2%, dragging the annual rate lower, which sets up a bullish unwind for gold, silver, and Bitcoin if the hikes never come, and Lark Davis relays Cathie Wood making the same call, inflation dropping toward 0 to 1% while the economy booms.

Underneath the argument, the rotation is already moving: per Joseph Hogue, more than $2 trillion has come off the Magnificent Seven in six weeks with $NVDA down 14% and $MSFT flagged as the highest-risk of the group, while cybersecurity ($PANW up 23% in a week) and defensives ($XLV, $XLP, $XLRE) lead.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $HIMS, the name that says GLP-1 beats AI. Per Prof G Markets, Hims and Hers CEO Andrew Dudum guides to about $3B in revenue this year, now partners directly with Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly selling their products at $150, and argues GLP-1s will do more for society over five years than AI. A July meeting on moving peptides to a category-1 list is the near catalyst. The stock is wildly volatile, so it is a watchlist name with a wide range, not a set-and-forget.
  • $SKHY, the memory IPO that lands Friday. Per the People-to-follow list, SK Hynix lists on the Nasdaq this Friday with a 91% DRAM gross margin last quarter, the second direct memory vehicle beside Micron. If you believe the invoices in section one, this is the freshest way to express it.
  • $CCXI, the first pure humanoid-robot ticker. Per the Stocks list, Churchill Capital XI is merging Agility Robotics at a $2.5B pre-money valuation with a $200M PIPE led by Foxconn, and Agility's Digit already logs Amazon and Toyota hours with $300M-plus in binding orders. Speculative, but it is the cleanest listed way to own the robotics demand layer Micron keeps citing.
  • $TSM, the one to buy on the ruling, not the record. Per Rick Orford, the stock hit a record near $476 then slid about 7%, and the pricing story is a multi-year tailwind. The move is to keep dry powder for a panic around the October ITC decision rather than chase the high.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Cross-routed to AI & Tools: the GLM 5.2 open-weight breakthrough and the Cloudflare pay-per-crawl toll road went there as the model and monetization stories, though $NET is the investable ticker on the latter. Cross-routed to News: the $JD plan to replace 700,000 workers, the EU MiCA compliance cliff, and Europe's defense-fund squeeze went there. Noted: per the Crypto list and confirmed on Kalshi, Nvidia launched a program letting AI startups swap compute for a share of future revenue, a structural shift in how the buildout gets funded. Noted: per the $JD tweet, a battery-backup-unit cell shortage is a fresh data-center bottleneck, with Samsung SDI, Panasonic, and Murata the cleanest reads and $VRT and $ETN the US adjacents. Noted: per Lark Davis, a Grayscale note argues Solana DeFi is undervalued against Ethereum, with Jupiter, Jito, and Meteora the sub-$1B names, treat as one analyst's pitch. Dropped: Degen Sensei (paywalled, no named levels), Invest with Henry (hype channel, unsupported odds), Trader Mayne and a Vox Day fiction serial (no signal).

item.title: [News] Khamenei's own son skipped his funeral, and China put a number on the robots taking the jobs: 700,000, with 120 schools to retrain the survivors
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: A missing heir hinted at a succession fight in Tehran, JD.com named the workforce it will automate away, and Europe spent the weekend discovering its money and its rules do not reach as far as it thought. Here is what moved after the AM edition.
item.content:encoded:
A missing heir hinted at a succession fight in Tehran, JD.com named the workforce it will automate away, and Europe spent the weekend discovering its money and its rules do not reach as far as it thought. Here is what moved after the AM edition.

The morning edition left Iran in a mourning pause and the robots as a projection. By evening both had a sharper edge: a succession signal from a funeral, and a company naming the exact number of jobs it will erase. Underneath, Europe had a quietly bad weekend on two fronts at once. Here is the update.

1. The missing mourner in Tehran is the story. Per the Polymarket list, Mojtaba Khamenei, long treated as a possible successor to his father, was reportedly absent from Ali Khamenei's funeral while the rest of Iran's top officials attended. In a system where presence at the funeral is a claim on power, an absence like that reads as friction inside the succession rather than around it. It advances this morning's thread, the US-granted mourning pause and the July 11 talks in Pakistan, by pointing at the question those talks will actually turn on: who Washington will be negotiating with a year from now. In a related move, per the same list, France is sending its carrier the Charles de Gaulle home after roughly two months stationed near the Strait of Hormuz, a quiet de-escalation signal from Europe.

2. China put a hard number on automation, and a retraining plan under it. Per a Nikkei-sourced report, JD.com CEO Liu Qiangdong says robots will replace about 700,000 of the company's delivery workers, and JD has signed with roughly 120 schools to retrain those workers into robot repair and maintenance roles. Beijing has reportedly started tracking AI's hit to employment as a national priority. The comparison that frames it is Amazon avoiding around 600,000 future hires through automation, and the read-through names for who follows next are DoorDash, Uber, and MercadoLibre. This is the concrete escalation of the morning's jobs story: not a warning about displacement, but a headcount, a timeline, and a plan for what happens to the people.

3. Europe found the limits of its money and its rules in the same weekend. Two stories rhymed. On rules, per the Milk Road letter, the MiCA transition window shut on July 1, and roughly 80% of about 1,200 EU crypto firms lack a compliant license and must now wind down, migrate, or leave, even as Brussels fast-tracks a digital euro. On money, per the Polymarket list, France drew 15.1 billion euros from the EU's SAFE defense fund against the 16.2 billion it requested after Franco-British missile projects were ruled ineligible under a 65% EU-origin rule, and Germany's cabinet is set to approve a budget with more than 203 billion euros in fresh borrowing while 20,000 protesters gathered at the AfD's annual conference. A continent trying to build sovereignty in defense and finance kept bumping into its own rulebook.

Around the world, quickly.

  • France. Per the Polymarket list, a court ordered luxury magnate Bernard Arnault to pay $25.73M in back taxes.
  • Hungary. Per the same list, lawmakers filed a bill seeking to oust President Tamas Sulyok, an Orban ally, a rare move against the ruling bloc.
  • Arabian Sea. The US Navy suspended its search for a sailor missing since July 1 after an MH-60S helicopter went down near the carrier George H.W. Bush.
  • Ukraine. Kyiv says Russia destroyed or damaged more than 200 railway locomotives across 2026, a slow attritional strike on the logistics network behind the front.
  • United States, 2028. Per the Kalshi list, Gavin Newsom leads the Democratic nomination market at 21%, down from a 37% high in November, and John James sits at 84% to win Michigan's GOP gubernatorial primary.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Cross-routed to Investing: the memory price hikes, the neocloud selloff, the SK Hynix IPO, and the Bitcoin outflow story all went there as market material. Cross-routed to AI & Tools: China's LongCat-2.0 open model, Alibaba banning Claude Code internally, and the two new Polymarket markets on the US pulling access to major AI models went there as the model-and-policy story. Dropped: a Vox Day fiction serial (no news), and a thin get-rich listicle with no argument.

item.title: [AI & Tools] The AI story this weekend was not Fable 5 coming back, it was a Chinese open model good enough to make Gavin Baker rethink his whole framework
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: GLM 5.2 landed as the first open-weight model credibly matching the US frontier, one engineer proved cheap models now tie the expensive ones on ordinary work, and the real lesson underneath both is that execution is getting free while imagination is not. Here is the shift.
item.content:encoded:
GLM 5.2 landed as the first open-weight model credibly matching the US frontier, one engineer proved cheap models now tie the expensive ones on ordinary work, and the real lesson underneath both is that execution is getting free while imagination is not. Here is the shift.

Fable 5 returning to subscriptions grabbed the headlines, but the durable story sits underneath it. A Chinese open-weight model reached the frontier, an experiment showed cheap models now match expensive ones on everyday work, and together they move the value in AI away from who executes and toward who knows what to ask. Here is what actually changed today.

1. GLM 5.2 is the model of the week, and it is not American. Per Jordi Visser, GLM 5.2 is the first Chinese open-weight model to credibly match or beat the US frontier, enough that Gavin Baker said it forced him to rethink his framework and one Databricks voice called it the Claude moment. Visser's nuance is the part to hold: the frontier still captures about 90% of the economic value even though open-source models process north of 80% of the tokens, so this is a margin threat, not a takeover, and it puts real pressure on the story that Anthropic grows revenue tenfold from here.

It did not arrive alone. Per the Hermes list, Meituan fully open-sourced LongCat-2.0 under an MIT license, a 1.6 trillion parameter mixture-of-experts model with a million-token context running on domestic chips. And per the Polymarket list, Alibaba banned its own employees from using Claude Code, while two fresh markets opened on whether the US removes public access to a major AI model, including a Chinese one, this year. The China-versus-US model axis is now the main axis.

2. Cheap models tie the expensive ones, so the value moved to imagination. The sharpest framing of the day. Per Nate B Jones, Mitchell Hashimoto ran ordinary feature work through a budget model for under a dollar in minutes, GPT-5.5 for about $1.50, and Fable 5 for about $9 and 40 minutes, all landing at the same quality, and everyone drew the obvious lesson to route cheap. The overlooked second test is the real one: Hashimoto handed the frontier model a gnarly systems optimization he designed himself, and it spent two hours and $40 reaching performance he says he could not have hit alone. The point is that no manager assigned that task, because it did not exist until an expert imagined it.

Execution is commoditizing, so the moat becomes the length of your list of things you know how to ask for. His leadership test is the quotable one: who on your team is allowed to pose a $400 question to a model today without asking permission? If the answer is nobody, that is an imagination limit, not a budget one.

3. If you keep Fable 5, stop giving it tasks and start giving it responsibilities. With Fable back on subscriptions only through 11:59pm Pacific on July 7, per the Hermes list, and then credits-only, how you drive it matters. Per Daria Cupareanu, the core shift, attributed to Anthropic's Claude Code lead Felix Rieseberg, is to give the model an outcome rather than a set of steps, because over-constraining boxes in the judgment that makes it good. The most concrete moves: tell it why the work matters and who it is for, do not default the effort dial to Max because High clears almost everything while Max burns quota for little gain, and make it verify its own output, which Anthropic's own team named the single highest-impact habit. One number worth filing: Fable runs at five times Sonnet 5's rate and twice Opus 4.8, and Anthropic admits it burns a weekly limit faster than any other model.

4. The tooling layer is quietly standardizing around agents. Two signals that the plumbing is maturing. Per Kun Chen, a principal engineer walked through a reproducible setup where the whole machine is declared in Nix so an agent that breaks something can be rebuilt in one script, all agents share a single global rules file symlinked into Claude, Codex, and the rest, and, most notably, he abandoned years of tmux for Herder, a terminal multiplexer built for the agent era that shows per-agent status natively and runs on Windows. Per the Hermes list, the open-agent side keeps shipping too, with the Nous Hermes agent exposing 47 slash commands and a growing skill-pack ecosystem, and Pliny releasing T3MP3ST, a multi-agent security swarm that turns a Claude Code or Codex subscription into an autonomous bug hunter claiming 90.1% on a benchmark where the incumbent scored 85%. That last one is a real dual-use flare to keep an eye on.

5. A new way to charge for AI, and a caution about swapping vendors. Two smaller shifts. Per Rick Orford, Cloudflare is building a toll booth for AI crawlers using the HTTP 402 payment-required response, with publishers now sending more than a billion payment notices a day and backers including the Associated Press, Reddit, and Pinterest, though Amazon rolled out a competing tool in June that is the real contest. And on the architecture-risk thread from this morning, per Kris Torrington, the warning is that a model can silently degrade while the dashboard stays green, and that is policy risk rather than model risk, with his line landing hard: if you move your whole stack from one dependency to another, you did not solve the problem, you just changed the logo on the risk.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Cross-routed to Investing: the memory price hikes, the neocloud selloff, TSMC repricing 2nm wafers, and the Cloudflare investable ticker $NET all went there. Noted here: per the People-to-follow list, Grok 4.5 is in private beta at Tesla and SpaceX on a 1.5 trillion parameter model, with Musk claiming it is close to or beyond Opus and xAI promising a new foundation model monthly for the rest of 2026. Dropped: a thin get-rich listicle and a one-minute clip on government-owned AI, though both echo the same quiet theme, that running local and open models is becoming the hedge against centralized-AI policy risk.

item.title: [Investing] Nvidia is sitting at a decade-low valuation while every overnight list screams that compute demand has never been higher
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The memory trade grew a twin this weekend as Jensen Huang mapped a path to a trillion in revenue, five hyperscalers committed $700 billion to build in 2026, and Bitcoin defended $60,000 for the fourth time since February. Here is the week ahead.
item.content:encoded:
The memory trade grew a twin this weekend as Jensen Huang mapped a path to a trillion in revenue, five hyperscalers committed $700 billion to build in 2026, and Bitcoin defended $60,000 for the fourth time since February. Here is the week ahead.

The overnight lists all pointed at the same gap. AI compute demand is running at numbers nobody has seen, and the stock most levered to it is trading like the story is over. That disconnect, not any single headline, is the thing to carry into Monday. Below it, the memory trade added new names, Bitcoin sat at one price with three different year-end targets stapled to it, and the money quietly rotated.

The week ahead. US markets were closed Friday and reopen Monday July 6. Four things to pre-load. First, the FOMC minutes land Wednesday July 8 at 2pm ET, and every line gets read for hike risk now that a July move is live. Second, the SK Hynix US listing is still on deck around July 10, a fresh pure-play memory bet in front of American money. Third, the levels: per Stocks with Josh, SPY needs a weekly close above 755 to confirm the next leg up and holds its trend only above 740, with a close below 740 opening an air pocket down toward 679. Fourth, the macro is genuinely two-sided now, because the same Kevin Warsh who put a July hike back on the table is also arguing that AI is deflationary, and Wednesday's minutes are where that fight shows up.

1. Nvidia at a decade-low valuation is the week's real tell. Per the Market list, $NVDA is sitting at fresh multi-year-low valuation against $MU, $INTC, and $AMD, with Shay Boloor flagging it as a decade low. Set that against what Jensen Huang is actually saying: per the People-to-follow list, a 1GW AI factory that costs about $55B today is heading toward $100B, roughly half of it flowing to Nvidia, against McKinsey's 156GW of AI demand by 2030 and a path to $1T in annual revenue.

The demand side is not a forecast anymore. Per the Crypto list, the five hyperscalers are spending $700B on AI infrastructure in 2026, six times their 2022 pace, and Wells Fargo models $2.47T of capex across 2026 to 2028. When the buyer of a company's chips is committing that much and the stock trades at a decade-low multiple, the market is telling you it does not believe the demand lasts. That is the bet to have a view on.

2. The memory trade grew a second and third name. Micron is no longer the whole story. Per the Stocks list, Gavin Baker is calling DRAM and HBM the single most important bottleneck in all of AI, with only three makers on Earth, $MU, SK Hynix, and Samsung, and Micron has now signed supply agreements covering about half its revenue at floor pricing already above last cycle's peak gross margins. The 2x Micron ETF ($DRAM, on Hyperliquid) is up 8 to 9% and above $65. The fresh wrinkle is where the extra upside sits: Marcos Milla argues $SNDK and SK Hynix have more room left than $MU, with Samsung the laggard, and that is why Leopold Aschenbrenner's book carries a 14.9% $SNDK weight next to his 35% $NBIS.

3. Bitcoin is defending $60,000 while three houses argue the target. Per the Kalshi list, Bitcoin reclaimed $63,000, with Kalshi Crypto pricing just a 15% chance it sees $100,000 this year. The bottom is the actual debate. Per the People-to-follow list, Michael Terpin notes BTC breached its 200-week average and touched 59K a week ago, then lays out the split: Standard Chartered sees the bottom at 59K and 100K by year end, while Galaxy sees the bottom in October at 40K to 46K.

Zach Pandl puts support near $58K and hangs the cyclical bottom on the Fed pausing and the CLARITY Act passing. VanEck's Matthew Sigel, per the Crypto list, wants clients at full positions by October. And per the Financial Markets list, Peter Brandt is contemplating selling some Bitcoin into gold, calling for gold to gain substantially on BTC. My read stays the same as Friday: this is a price bear market on a fundamentals bull market, and the accumulation zone is below $60,000, not chasing $69,000.

4. The rotation under the tape is the part nobody is tweeting. Per the Financial Markets list, tech stocks are on track for the largest annual inflow in history while energy just saw a $3.2B outflow, the largest in two years, and semiconductors are flashing as very expensive. The St. Louis Fed adds the structural number: information-processing and data-center construction now make up about a third of all US business investment, the highest share since 1947, and the producer price index including semiconductors is up 19% from January to May. Per the Kalshi list, Chinese chip exports to Hong Kong hit a record $15B in May, up over 200% since early 2025. The money is voting for compute and against everything else, and that is exactly the kind of one-way positioning that gets violent when it turns.

Single names worth your watchlist.

  • $NBIS, the neocloud dip everyone is calling a gift. Per the Crypto list, Wayne Liang is buying the $NBIS, $IREN, $CRWV, and $CIFR selloff, arguing the $META excess-compute narrative behind it is simply false with enterprise AI adoption still under 30%. High-beta, but the contracts are real.
  • $SNDK, the memory name with the most room left. Per the Stocks list, Sandisk carries more upside than Micron on Marcos Milla's read and is Aschenbrenner's second-largest position at 14.9%. It sits up 1,781% over three years on the S&P 10x leaderboard, second only to Applovin.
  • $UBER, the super-app that actually earns. Per the Stocks list, Uber runs about $28 of operating profit per user at roughly 28x, with over $10B committed across about 30 autonomous partners and robotaxis targeting 15-plus cities by end of 2026. The profit-per-user frame is the cleaner way to own the platforms than the multiple alone.
  • $TEM and $LLY, the AI-biotech thesis to keep on a back burner. Per Lark Davis, Dario Amodei's biotech-renaissance comments point him at Tempus AI applying models to cancer data and Eli Lilly's pipeline. It is a thesis with no levels attached yet, so it is a name to watch, not a trade.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Cross-routed to News: the $JD plan to replace 700,000 delivery workers with robots and BMW's humanoid rollout, as the automation-and-jobs story. Cross-routed to AI & Tools: the Fable 5 export ban and the route-around to Chinese models GLM 5.2 and Kimi. Cross-routed to News: the heat wave driving New England wholesale power up 243%, with the grid angle noted. Dropped: the threadguy home-robot livestream clip carried no ticker or thesis. Dropped: the memecoin shilling ($YzY, $ANSEM, the Andrew Tate meme revival), the prop-firm and paywall promos, and the July 4 sports and novelty markets, all noise.

item.title: [News] Trump handed Iran a week off for Khamenei's funeral, and the robots came for 700,000 jobs in a single announcement
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: US-Iran talks resume July 11 in Pakistan and Netanyahu heads to the White House, Ukrainian drones reached 850 kilometers inside Russia, and JD.com said it will replace 700,000 delivery workers with machines. The AI jobs story stopped being a projection.
item.content:encoded:
US-Iran talks resume July 11 in Pakistan and Netanyahu heads to the White House, Ukrainian drones reached 850 kilometers inside Russia, and JD.com said it will replace 700,000 delivery workers with machines. The AI jobs story stopped being a projection.

Independence Day weekend gave the feed a split screen: a careful pause in one war and fresh strikes deep inside another, a president clearing 702 rules while his rival warned about democracy, and the clearest sign yet that automation is now a headcount decision, not a thought experiment. Here is what actually moved.

1. The US gave Iran a week off, and the calendar now has real dates on it. Per the Polymarket list, Trump revealed the US granted Iran a pause for the funeral of the late Ayatollah Khamenei, with no shooting until the funeral events conclude, and formal US-Iran talks resume July 11 in Pakistan covering sanctions, frozen Iranian assets, and the nuclear file. Alongside it, Netanyahu is reportedly headed to the White House as early as next week. This is the diplomacy that yesterday's Strait of Hormuz brinkmanship was building toward, and the fact that both sides agreed to a mourning pause is itself the signal that neither wants the shooting war right now.

2. Ukraine reached 850 kilometers into Russia while the Kremlin said nothing has changed. Per the Polymarket list, Ukrainian drones struck Russian oil sites more than 850 kilometers inside Russia on Saturday and destroyed a MiG-29 at the Belbek airbase in Crimea. The Kremlin's Dmitry Peskov reaffirmed the war continues and said Zelensky is welcome to come to Moscow, while Dmitry Medvedev framed the Strait of Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb as Iran's non-nuclear weapons. The reach of those strikes is the story: the war's front line is now wherever a cheap drone can fly, and Russian oil infrastructure hundreds of miles from Ukraine is inside it.

3. The AI jobs story went from projection to headcount. Per the Market list, JD.com CEO Liu Qiangdong warned that robots will replace 700,000 of the company's delivery workers, and the company has signed with about 120 schools to retrain those workers into robot-repair roles, with Beijing now tracking the AI jobs hit as a national priority. It echoes the earlier Amazon figure of 600,000 future hires avoided, and per the Financial Markets list, BMW is already deploying humanoid robots at its South Carolina factory. This is the concrete version of the labor-market crack the weekend memo traced: not a statistic about displacement, but a company naming the number of jobs and the schools it will send those people to instead.

4. Trump moved to cut 702 federal regulations. Per the Kalshi list, the administration announced plans to eliminate 702 federal regulations. On the same July 4 tape, Bill Clinton used a holiday statement to warn that America faces serious threats to its democracy, and Zelensky said he had a very good call with Trump. The deregulation push is the substantive policy move under the holiday noise, and it is the one to track for what actually gets struck.

5. A heat wave is stress-testing the grid, and the power price is the tell. Per the People-to-follow list, extreme heat is pushing the central and eastern US toward 105°F, and Thursday's wholesale power spot prices jumped hard: New England up 243%, New York City up 101%, the Midwest up 55%, and the Mid-Atlantic up 45.6%, with PJM placed under a federal alert to cut usage. The AI-and-cooling load and the summer heat are hitting the same grid at the same time, and the wholesale price is where that collision shows up first. The investable side of this, the power and grid names, sits in Investing.

Around the world, quickly.

  • Venezuela. Per the Polymarket list, the death toll from the June 24 earthquakes rose to 2,954 dead and 16,592 injured, per Parliament President Jorge Rodríguez.
  • Germany. AfD leader Alice Weidel claimed most migrants will return home once they stop receiving money from the German state, sharpening the same benefits-versus-work line that ran through the weekend's immigration debate.
  • Australia. New South Wales confirmed its first human H5N1 bird-flu case, a marker worth tracking rather than alarm.
  • Indonesia. Per the Financial Markets list, the country jailed entrepreneur Nadiem Makarim, the Gojek founder and former education minister, for roughly a decade.
  • Paul Pelosi. Per the Polymarket list, he reportedly faces a hit-and-run charge after allegedly striking a parked car and driving away.
  • America 250, the messy version. Severe storms cleared the National Mall before Trump's speech, which slid to 11pm, and the Brooklyn Bridge briefly caught fire during New York's fireworks. The anniversary went off, just not on script.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Cross-routed to Investing: Kevin Warsh's argument that AI is deflationary, as a macro and rate story. Cross-routed to Investing: the record $15B in Chinese chip exports to Hong Kong. Dropped: the America250 "250 years of predictions" series, the Joey Chestnut hot-dog title, the Ben Franklin look-alike contest, and the World Cup announcer and tailgate novelty markets, all holiday filler. Dropped: the Elon and Tesla July 4 promo quote-cards, no news.

item.title: [AI & Tools] The Fable 5 ban taught the real lesson this weekend: one vendor in one country is one point of failure
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: A clean post-mortem of the export shutdown lands as the usage numbers go vertical, Nous Research turns Hermes into an operating layer, and the pros settle on Fable-plans-Codex-executes as the default. Build so a policy change is a config change.
item.content:encoded:
A clean post-mortem of the export shutdown lands as the usage numbers go vertical, Nous Research turns Hermes into an operating layer, and the pros settle on Fable-plans-Codex-executes as the default. Build so a policy change is a config change.

The weekend's best AI writing was not about a new model. It was about what the Fable 5 outage actually proved, and the answer is an architecture warning every builder should act on. Underneath it, the demand data went vertical, the open-source camp shipped, and the practitioners quietly agreed on how to run these models. Here is the shift.

1. The real lesson of the ban is single vendor, single jurisdiction, single failure point. The cleanest post-mortem of the shutdown, a breakdown of whether the US could ban open-source AI, walks the timeline: Anthropic released Fable 5 and its lighter-guardrail twin Mythos 5 on June 9, an Amazon researcher found a narrow way to bypass Fable's safeguards on vulnerability prompts, and on June 12 the US applied export controls ordering Anthropic to restrict foreign-national access. Because nationality could not be verified in real time, both models went dark for everyone.

The key clarification is what did not happen: no open-weight model was pulled from Hugging Face, no repo was wiped. Frontier AI is now treated like advanced chips, where the lever is access, not quality. The takeaway is a rule, not a vibe: any revenue-critical or customer-facing workflow needs a fallback across vendor, jurisdiction, and deployment mode, because the failure can be silent, your dashboard green while the model underneath is quietly weaker or gated.

2. The route-around went straight to Chinese models. The same breakdown notes that when Fable went dark, builders did not wait. Some dropped to cheaper models, some went local, and a real share started testing the Chinese open models GLM 5.2 and Kimi, which are lower cost with an open door, accepting the different jurisdiction and trust profile as the price of continuity. That is the uncomfortable second-order effect of an export control aimed at denying access: it hands the open Chinese labs a live audition with exactly the builders the control was meant to keep on domestic frontier models.

3. The demand data went vertical, and that is why the labs can afford all this. Per the Crypto list, OpenRouter token volume ran from 6.4T in the first week of January to 14.8T weekly by March, up 160% in two months, and H100 rental prices, after falling for two years, reversed and climbed 40% in five months to $2.35. Enterprise AI adoption is still under 30% by the same desk's estimate, and that is the bull and the warning at once: the usage curve is near vertical and most of the enterprise has not even started. The tokens are real, and that is the ground truth under every capex headline.

4. Nous Research is turning Hermes into an operating layer. Per the Hermes list, the open-agent camp shipped all weekend. Co-founder Teknium announced an expanded plugin interface so developers with long-pending pull requests can ship stable plugins without merge dependency, Tony Simons pushed the Hermes A2A Bridge to v0.4.8 with local agent-to-agent interop, and DODOREACH wired a robodog as the first physical Hermes pet with spontaneous text-to-speech. Kevin Simback's Hermes Atlas newsletter frames the thesis plainly, that Hermes is becoming an operating layer for AI work positioned for the sovereign and open-source movement. This is the counterweight to section one: the answer to single-vendor risk is partly the open stack maturing into something you can actually run yourself.

5. The pros have settled on a division of labor, and it is Fable plans, Codex executes. Per the Hermes list, Matthew Berman is paying $200 a month each for two services and splitting the work, Fable for planning and GPT-5.5 for execution. Theo from t3.gg posted his own config, Fable plans and Codex executes, calling Codex far better at computer use, UI verification, and executing well-specified work, and noting he was throwing away about half his agent-driven pull requests before the workflow and now closes almost none. The lesson from yesterday's restraint argument lands here as a concrete setup: use the strongest model to decide what done looks like, then hand a tightly-scoped build to the executor.

Filtered / Cross-Routed. Cross-routed to Investing: the $700B hyperscaler capex and the neocloud selloff, as the trade. Noted here but light on specifics: Polymarket prices a 62% chance the next Gemini Pro model ships July 17 to match Anthropic's Mythos, and per the Crypto list Anthropic's Mythos tool is being used by Coinbase and Binance to stress-test infrastructure even as a Vercel-adjacent breach exposed crypto-dev API keys, a reminder the agent security surface is widening. Dropped: the Nate B Jones "Fable 5 wants the whole job" clip returned only a fragment with no new specifics beyond yesterday's restraint theme.

item.title: [Investing] The memory trade got a bear, a structural bull, and a $500 Trump-basket receipt on the same holiday tape
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Burry is shorting Micron on nothing but a chart while Shkreli and Aschenbrenner argue the supply is structurally locked, the Fed conversation quietly flipped from when-they-cut to might-they-hike, and Bitcoin is being bought and doubted at the exact same $62,500.
item.content:encoded:
Burry is shorting Micron on nothing but a chart while Shkreli and Aschenbrenner argue the supply is structurally locked, the Fed conversation quietly flipped from when-they-cut to might-they-hike, and Bitcoin is being bought and doubted at the exact same $62,500.

Nothing traded today and nothing trades tomorrow, so this is the second holiday session to decide what you believe before Monday can price it for you. Three arguments hardened over the afternoon. The memory trade split into a clean bull-bear fight with names on both sides, the rate debate flipped from cuts to a live hike, and Bitcoin sat at one price with the accumulators and the doubters both pointing at it.

The week ahead, and the one thing that changed since this morning. US markets were closed Friday July 3 for Independence Day and stayed closed for the weekend, reopening Monday July 6. Five things to pre-load, and one of them is new since the AM. First and biggest: the Fed conversation is no longer only about cuts. Per the Market list, Polymarket now prices the odds of a rate hike at roughly a coin flip, up from near zero earlier this year, with Chris Waller already calling for one and new chair Kevin Warsh refusing to rule out July. Second, the FOMC minutes land Wednesday at 2pm ET and every line now gets read for hike risk, not just cut timing.

Three more to pre-load. The SK Hynix US listing is still on deck around July 10, a fresh pure-play memory bet in front of American money. $SPCX joins the Nasdaq-100 on July 7. And a record pile of money-market cash still sits on the sideline waiting for one of these to break.
